lead: Moving house is often listed among life's most stressful events, and after two decades of helping Suffolk families pack up and start afresh, we understand exactly why. The good news is that almost all of that worry is avoidable. A calm move is not luck; it is the result of a handful of sensible habits put in place early. In this guide our family team shares the practical, reassuring advice we offer our own customers across Ipswich and beyond.

sections.h: Why Starting Early Takes the Pressure Off
sections.body:
If there is one thing we have learned since 2005, it is that a relaxed move and an early start are the same thing. The families who feel in control on the big day are almost always the ones who began thinking about it weeks rather than days beforehand. Time is the single most generous gift you can give yourself, because it turns a frantic scramble into a series of small, manageable jobs.

When you start early, problems surface while there is still room to solve them. You discover that the loft holds more than you remembered, or that a wardrobe will not fit down the stairs in one piece, and you have the breathing space to deal with it properly. We see the difference at the door: an organised household greets the van with a cup of tea ready, while a last-minute one greets it still taping boxes.

Starting early also helps emotionally. A move is a big change, and giving yourself time to adjust, to say goodbye to a home and look forward to the next one, makes the whole experience feel less like an upheaval and more like a fresh chapter you have chosen.

sections.h: Building a Realistic Timeline You Can Actually Follow
sections.body:
A timeline only works if it reflects real life, not a fantasy version of your week. We always suggest sketching one out as soon as a moving date looks likely, working backwards from the day itself. Six to eight weeks ahead is an ideal moment to begin, even if the date is not yet set in stone, because the early tasks rarely depend on a firm completion.

Keep it simple. In the first weeks, focus on decluttering and gathering boxes. A fortnight out, begin packing the rooms you barely use, such as the spare room or the garage. In the final week, tackle the kitchen and the everyday items, leaving only the essentials for the last day. Spreading the work this way means no single evening ever feels overwhelming.

Write everything down somewhere you will see it, whether that is a notebook on the kitchen counter or a shared note on your phone. In our experience, the act of ticking off small jobs does wonders for morale and stops that nagging feeling that something important has been forgotten.

sections.h: Declutter Before You Pack a Single Box
sections.body:
Here is a truth worth repeating: it is far cheaper, quicker and calmer to move fewer things. Every box you pack is a box you must carry, transport and unpack at the other end, so there is real sense in being honest about what deserves to come with you. We gently encourage every customer to declutter before packing, never during, because mid-pack is exactly when good judgement deserts you.

Go room by room and be practical rather than ruthless. If something has not been used or worn in a year, ask whether it earns its place in the new home. Charity shops around Ipswich and the Suffolk market towns are always glad of good-quality items, and a quick trip to the recycling centre clears the genuine rubbish. What remains is a lighter, leaner load that costs less to move and unpacks far more easily.

Decluttering also gives you a clearer picture of how much you are actually moving, which helps when we plan the right van and crew for the job. A home that has been thinned out sensibly almost always moves faster and more smoothly on the day.

Good packing is less about speed and more about order. We always advise working one room at a time and finishing it completely before moving on, rather than drifting between spaces and ending up with half-filled boxes everywhere. Heavy items such as books go into small boxes, while lighter, bulkier things like bedding and cushions fill the larger ones. This keeps every box liftable and protects backs all round.

Labelling is where many people cut corners, and it is the very thing that decides how stressful the first evening will be. Write the room and a short note of the contents on the top and at least one side of each box, so it can be read whichever way it sits in the van. A box marked simply "kitchen, plates and mugs" tells our crews exactly where to set it down and tells you exactly where to look later.

Wrap fragile items individually and cushion them well, and do not be tempted to overfill a box just to use fewer of them. A box that closes flat and feels secure will travel far better than a bulging one. If packing feels like too much alongside everything else, it is a service we are always happy to take off your hands entirely.

sections.h: The Essentials Box and Keeping Moving Day Calm
sections.body:
If we could insist on one thing, it would be the essentials box, sometimes called the first-night box. This is the box that goes in the car with you, not the van, and it carries everything you will reach for in the first few hours at the new house. Kettle, mugs, tea and biscuits, of course, but also phone chargers, a change of clothes, medication, toiletries, a torch and the all-important toilet roll.

Moving day itself runs far more smoothly when a few small details are arranged in advance. Sort out parking for the removal van the day before, especially if you are near a busy junction or off one of the lanes that feed onto the A12 or A14, so the team can load and unload close to the door. If you have children or pets, consider whether a friend or relative could look after them for the day; a calm dog and a happy child make for a calmer move all round.

Keep the kettle accessible and offer the crew a cup of tea early on. Beyond simple good manners, it sets a friendly, unhurried tone, and an unhurried move is a smooth one. With the essentials to hand and the day's logistics sorted, the actual hours of moving become surprisingly relaxed.

The temptation once everything is inside is to unpack the entire house by bedtime. Resist it. After a long day, the kindest thing you can do is set up the beds, find the kettle and the bathroom essentials, and leave the rest until morning. A good night's sleep in your new home matters far more than an empty hallway.

Unpack in the order you live, not the order the boxes came in. The kitchen and the bedrooms first, then the living spaces, and the lofts and spare rooms last of all. Because you labelled everything clearly, you can find exactly what you need without tearing through sealed boxes, and the house comes together at a pace that feels comfortable rather than frantic.

Do not forget the quieter admin that helps a move feel truly finished. Redirect your post, update your address with the council, banks and the doctor, register the children at their new school, and let the utility providers know you have moved in. Tackling a couple of these each day in the first week, rather than all at once, keeps the loose ends from becoming a source of stress just when you should be enjoying the new place.

faqs: How far in advance should we start planning a house move?

We suggest beginning around six to eight weeks before your expected moving date, even before it is firmly fixed. Early tasks such as decluttering and gathering packing materials do not depend on a confirmed completion, and starting them sooner turns a stressful rush into a series of small, manageable jobs.
What should go in an essentials or first-night box?

Pack the things you will need in the first few hours at your new home and keep this box in your own car rather than the van. We recommend a kettle, mugs, tea and biscuits, phone chargers, any medication, toiletries, a change of clothes, a torch and toilet roll, so you can settle in without hunting through sealed boxes.
How do we make moving day easier with children or pets?

In our experience, the calmest days are the ones where children and pets are looked after elsewhere if possible. Asking a friend or relative to mind them for the day keeps everyone relaxed and the doorways clear. If they must stay, set up a quiet room away from the loading and unloading so they feel safe.
Should we declutter before or while we pack?

Always before. Sorting through your belongings first means you only pack and pay to move the things you actually want, which saves time, effort and cost. Decluttering mid-pack tends to slow everything down and clouds your judgement, so we encourage every customer to thin things out room by room before a single box is filled.
